speaker: interviewee
text: It was that Saturday when they came back and told us about the order he had. But apparently he ripped up the order, in front of the prison elder and says whoever issued that order has  no [? moral ?] [? force. ?] It does me nothing. And I'm not going to bloody my hands in the last days of  the war to kill you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So whatever food in the camp is, use it up, eat. You should be liberated within days, a matter of two,  three days. That went on Sunday, Monday. Tuesday morning, which was May  the 8th, we still had roll call. When we came out to role call, we see all the Nazi guards, the SS, assembled, with full gear, ready to march off. The chief Nazi comes in. He takes the reports. How many prisoners? And turns around, walks out. So the prison elder says, fellow prisoners, today, we are being liberated. Our camp commander was gracious enough to leave us two guards, which were volunteers, to stay with us for our own protection.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Russians should be here about 2 o'clock in the afternoon. But in between, there will  be individual units of SS marching by. And if they see an unguarded camp, they may murder us in the last  minute. And it would be a shame. So he offered us those guards, that one. Of course, those guards were decent fellows that never hurt anybody. And they volunteered to stay with us. They stayed with us till about noon. And then they changed into prison garb, as prisoners, with us. And those were that nobody would notice them or hurt them, because they were decent fellows, truly. One of them had the order to give lashes to somebody. He told the kapo to hit the bench and not the prisoner and told the prisoner to scream. And those are the guys that wanted to stay with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly around 2 o'clock, the first Russian tank appeared. It did not bother to stop to open the gates to the camp. He just went right through the camp, broke them down, the gates, and went  in. Of course, there was a tremendous hurrah. There was euphoria. We have free. We were given 24 hours of freedom of the city. Go into any unoccupied building or house for that matter. If  the door is locked, break it down. Get clothing. Get food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's exactly what we did. Everybody discarded their uniforms, which were filthy, dirty. And we got dressed. We looked like a bunch of clowns, the sight  of it, with hats and everything, whatever you-- you imagine it. [INAUDIBLE] I remember removing my number from my prison camp and sticking it in my pocket. I said, that's got to be souvenir. I'll keep it with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, euphoria passes. Within a day or two, reality began to-- to sink in. What now? We are liberated. Who else was liberated? Did any survive? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in that town for a few weeks-- well, three to four weeks. And I decided to go back to my hometown. Perhaps somebody of my family survived. When I reached town, I found my brother next to me, Jacob, survived. He was sick, walking around with hot pads on his back, apparently his kidneys, which I didn't know at that  time. He couldn't even straighten out, was constantly simply sick. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Poland was no have for the surviving Jews at that time. I'll give you the highlights of it. I'll just mention two episodes that happened to me. One, which happened within a couple of months after arriving in my hometown, I'm walking down the street. I meet a couple of fellow Jews, young Jews, on the street. And in those days, when you met a fellow Jew, it was like meeting another brother. We hugged and started talking. Well, they-- they made the fatal mistake of beginning to speak Yiddish to me. And three hoodlums walking by the street, by the way, oh, Jews still survive. What a pity Hitler didn't kill them-- one  word to another. When they were bypassing us, one shoves the next one to him against me and shoulders me. And being that I was very hot-tempered, I just grabbed him by the scruff of his neck, by the shirt collar. And said to him, you son of a bitch. One more word our of you, and I kill you. And he turned around and says, let go, you rotten Jew, to me. He said that, and I lost all control of me and slapped him with the open palm of my hand. And I slapped him so hard that, with  that single blow, I knocked him out. When I dropped him, he just fell like a piece of food to the ground. [INAUDIBLE] I could see blood oozing from his nose  and from his lips. I cut his lips through right against his teeth. I hit him. And his left eye was closed up, too, just lying. The other two, his buddies, were stunned. They started screaming at the top of their lungs, help, help. A Jew killed a Polack. Within seconds, I was surrounded. Because that was a crossing. I'm standing with guy at my feet. And there are about 100 people over there, woman, men. And there someone starts screaming, get some water. They bring him some water. They revived the guy, propped him up. Kill the Jew, the cry from the crowd comes up. Give him a stone. Somebody shoves a half a brick into his hand. And he starts-- he's like drunk, but starts walking towards me with the half  a brick. And I became like a caged animal at the time. I was a desperado. I didn't-- reckless. I didn't care what happens to me. But I started walking towards him with my fists, ready to kill him. I says, drop that stone. If not this time, I'll kill you for sure. You won't get up anymore. And  I meant it, too. I probably would have killed him. The guy got scared. And he starts backing up, one step after another. And he dropped that stone. I repeated that. And he says to me, I'll catch up with another time. I says, anytime, anyplace, you name it time. I'm ready for you. And his buddies take  him by his arms. They walk off.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, like a crazy nut, turn around to the crowd. I say, well, anybody else want to try to kill the Jew? But one thing I promise you, at least two of you will be down with me before I go down. Because Hitler didn't kill him. And that Jew doesn't kill so easy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody moves. As fast as they came, as fast they dispersed. There are two guys on the corner, in their hats, standing, come over to me, touch me on the shoulder and stretches out to shake my hand. Bravo, comrade, he says. Good show. We were just watching. If somebody moved in on you, we would deal with them harshly. He lifts his lapel and shows  me his insignia. He's from the Secret Service, the police. And he opens the jacket, and there is the sidearm, the revolver on him. And I think to myself, you bastard. You would be  probably the first one to put a bullet into me saying that I caused a disturbance. I left. This time, I was resigned to-- to leave Poland. For sure, it's no place for me. I also remembered, at that time, we had relatives in the United States, an uncle, my mother's brother, Grossman. I remembered the city. I remembered the street where he lived  in Baltimore, Maryland. On the-- but I did not remember their number, neither did my brother. Anyways, I wrote a letter to him, that I'm in Poland, but I will be leaving for Western Germany, that I survived and a brother  of mine. My brother is sick. But we'll try and get into Western Germany. Perhaps other members of our family survived. Within a month,  I received a reply, a letter from-- the letter reached him even  though I didn't have the number of the street. He has good news for me. He knows that a sister of mine survived. He heard from an American GI, who liberated her. She was liberated in Bohemia, Czechoslovakia. But she was sick. He forwarded some money to the GI. But he didn't hear  any more from them, because they pulled out and the Russians took over the rest of Czechoslovakia. And the Americans moved back. It didn't take till January '46. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went with the group from the Mizrahi, which was a Jewish organization in the neighboring town which had organized all the survivors, young survivors, to smuggle them out of Poland hopefully to get them into Palestine. I joined that group, went on the border. We crossed the border into Czechoslovakia. But the Czech authorities picked us  up, and they returned us to Poland. Of course, we went with false passports. We were supposed to be Greek citizens. None of us spoke a word of Polish. So among ourselves, we used whatever Hebrew words we knew. We used just those Hebrew words not having any meaning, you know. And the guards couldn't detect  us. They finally broke one of the prisoners down, one of us, a girl, you know. He tricked her into something. The same with me. He asked me to get out. And I looked at him, not understanding a word. And I say to him, in Hebrew, [HEBREW], you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he took me by the arm and points to the door to go. And the guard, when he said the same thing to her, she moved. So, oh, so you understood Polish. And that was-- our number was up.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us to a jail in Rybnik, where we spent five weeks in a jail. It was a court. And, of course, the court, the judge was bribed, whether through the American Joint that sent in money or whatever. After five weeks, we were released from jail. I even have a picture that I can show you when I came out of jail. I was as fat as can be. Because jail, in Poland, after the concentration camp, was a Sunday school. I think for all the Polish prisoners there, I knew the latest Polish songs, from the hit parade, from the Polish. And I sang for them. And I had plenty of food, even to drink and to smoke. So I was a prince, there in jail. [LAUGHS] I got out of jail. We tried, again, to cross the border into Czechoslovakia. And that was the 3rd of May, 1946. We were to assemble in Krakow to meet another group and to go to southern Poland, cross the mountains into Czechoslovakia. Well, we arrived 50 minutes later-- too late, rather, in Krakow. That was my luck and our group's luck that we are alive. Because the other group left with us, a group of 25. One survived. The Polish underground, the fascist groups, what they called [NON-ENGLISH] or [NON-ENGLISH]. They were the extreme right. And they used to murder Jews even during the war. When the partisans, wherever they found a Jew, a Jewish partisan or in the wood, in hiding, they would murder him, too, even though they were fighting the Nazis. They say there is  an old Arabic proverb that says the enemy of my enemy is my friend. But that did not apply where the Polish nationalists, the right, and the Jews were concerned. That saying did not apply. Even though they were enemies, we had a common enemy, they would still murder the Jews. Anyway, those Jews were captured and were all massacred. I was at the burial. The next day, the farmers brought them in. I witnessed the bodies, covered with tarpaulins. We buried them in Krakow's Jewish cemetery. Everyone that was at that cemetery was crying at that  time. People that lived through hell on Earth were murdered now by the Poles. The next time we tried was in 19-- about two months later. They said, we got to go through a beaten route. And that meant going to Berlin. It costs more money, but we went to Berlin. A Russian officer took us in a truck right across the Polish border into Berlin. Those days, Berlin had no borders yet between the districts, the American, Russian, or British, or French. It was open in '46. We came into Berlin, stayed there a couple of weeks. With the next transport, we went into Western Germany. I came in to Hanover and from there, to Bavaria, to Munich. From there, we were sent to a DP camp. The DP camp was in Bad Reichenhall, which was right on the Austrian border. Immediately, I sent out letters to every capital in Europe, to the Red Cross, searching for my sister. I had a sister who was liberated, then in '45, in May '45, in Czechoslovakia. It took almost a year. It was the beginning of '47 when I had the first reply from a small town in Czechoslovakia. Yes, she was there. But she was  sent somewhere else. She left.   She was sick. Where she  went, she don't know. Within a couple  of weeks, I received another letter from a Jewish community in Pisek, which is also in Bohemia. Yes, she came in on that and that day. On that and that day, she went to the hospital, in Strakonice, where she died in the beginning of June. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I knew--

speaker: interviewee
text: One month later. A month after the liberation. A month after the liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: A month after liberation, she died. And it was typhus-- typhus fever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you told me that your brother-- the brother, surviving, died.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother died a month before I came to Canada. He was constantly sick, also, the kidneys. He was taken-- he had one attack after another. Finally, he was taken, operated on. And he got blood poison, and he died. He's buried in Munich, in the Jewish cemetery. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In 1948 on June 23, I arrived in Canada. I came through the auspices of the Canadian Jewish Congress. The Canadian government permitted 1,000 Jewish orphans to get into Canada. Even though I was already older than I was supposed to be-- of course, it was up to 18 that you could be classified as an orphan. So I changed the date of my birth, which was a cinch to do in Germany, from 1927 to 1930. I still figure on those papers. I never re-changed them. I never bothered, because it was too much hassle to go through. I changed to 1930, and I registered. And within months, I arrived in Canada. I was sent to Sydney, Nova Scotia, where I stayed with a Jewish family. And I worked for them. The family's name was Leavitton. They were very nice people. I can say that I have nothing but fond memories of Sydney. I spend there one year. When I came to Sydney, I spoke a bit of English. There were about six or eight of us that were sent to the area. And most of them were beginners. So they went to night school. When I went to night school the first night, and the teacher found out that I spoke a bit of English, and I became like the chief interpreter for the group.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he says, no, it's a shame to hold you back. So they gave me a private teacher for myself, a Scottish lady, a spinster. I remember, her name was, Miss. Spencer. So she was supposed to give me twice-weekly, hour lessons. She used to keep me two and three hours. I guess she enjoyed as much teaching me as I enjoyed learning English.

speaker: interviewee
text: After a year, she came out, she says, Roman, you just finished-- as far as composition and spelling, you finished grade 11. Vocabulary, perhaps grade 12. Because you know a tremendous amount by knowing several languages, she says, any new word I use to come out, you  know it. You know the meaning of it. So you did excellent. She told me, I have good news for you. You know, I belong to a lady's club. I believe it was called Daughters of the Empire, which they have quite common clubs on the East Coast. And you're always a topic of our meetings. All there are interested in you, how you're progressing and so forth. We have good news. If you stay in Sydney and you want to go to school, to continue study, we will sponsor you. We'll pay your tuition. So at the time, I almost had tears in my eyes. I said, Miss Spencer, I'm really touched by that. This, in my native country, would be unheard of for a Christian organization trying to support a  Jewish boy to put him through school. I am very grateful for it. But I have to decline the offer, because I will be leaving Sydney. I want to see the rest of Canada. Besides, Sydney is a small town. It's too small for me. I have outgrown it. At the time I left for Montreal, where I spent almost a year. It's not uneventful year, Montreal, working here and there. From Montreal, I moved to Toronto, where I landed the first job for Laboratories and Plastics. I worked there for   a while, close to a year, where I reached the--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is now around 1950.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was already 1950, '50 and '51. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: And after [? Reliable? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: --stayed. And '51, '52, I moved to Welland, Ontario. I had a friend living in Niagara Falls. I moved to Welland. I started in sales there, selling door to door. Eventually, I landed a job as a manager in the store. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: How and when did you meet your wife? How and when did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my wife in May '57.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a blind date from a mutual friend. I used to live in Welland, so always going to Toronto, which I was traveling practically weekly, coming down to Toronto. On Sundays, I would go down or sometimes during the week if I had business to attend. There was a stopover on the way, on number 20 Highway outside of Smithville. There was a Jewish farmer which I befriended. And that was my second home.

speaker: interviewee
text: The week I didn't show up, they used to question me. Where were you? We were worried that maybe you were sick or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to stopover in their place going to Toronto and  on the way back. I used to have dinner with them. It was a home-cooked meal, not a restaurant. So a fellow that was engaged to their daughter gave me the number of my future wife, of Miriam, to call her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I kept on putting it off till one Sunday. I came down. And before leaving, I picked up the phone. And I says to a friend of mine that lived in Toronto, I says, a fellow there gave me a number to call a girl here. I said I don't-- so my friend says, for God's sake. Like, his wife says-- Call her.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Roman, give her a call. Give the girl a break. So I called, the line-- line was busy. I wanted to leave already. She says, wait a minute. You are  not leaving my house till you get her  on the  phone. Sure enough, I phoned. And I made a date with her. She answered. She accepted the date.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the rest is history.

speaker: interviewee
text: I picked her up. It so happened that that night-- she was born on to the 21st of May. That was the nigh of 20th of May. When we got back, from our date, from the show, from the restaurant, it was already after 12:00. So I was the first one to wish her a happy birthday. And that was it. From then on, you know, date after date. The rest is history. We were married--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --almost a year later, not quite a year after we met. We were married on the 20th of April, 1958.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And you have three kids and two grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kids, my first son was born on January 22.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stuart. Of course, it was a happy event in my life, having a son, to have a name, which I gave him the name of my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I call him after my father and my wife's father, after my father, Simcha. It's a Hebrew name. Then we had another daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Debbie.

speaker: interviewee
text: Debbie was born in 1962. And then there was the baby, the last daughter, Adrienne, which was born  in 1964.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I worked hard at various jobs, sales and things. But I put them through school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I always emphasized the importance of education. And they listened to me. I put them through university. Stuart graduated with a BA from Carleton University. Debbie graduated from the University of Windsor, a BA in social work. Then she went for another year, where she got her master's degree in social work from Boston University. And Adrienne graduated from U of T.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you talked to your kids about all your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: To a degree. I did not want to put too much emphasis on the suffering, I think. But they had a certain degree of knowledge.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me that your-- your view in the war was to [INAUDIBLE] survive, so you could tell the world. But you didn't want to tell them too much?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want-- I felt that they were too young when they were children--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to tell them. When they used to ask me questions, how come they have no uncles or aunts or grandparents on my-- on my side? They had a grandmother and a step-grandfather, because Miriam's mother remarried here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I used to explain to them. It was a war. And they were killed. And I explained them-- well, later, when they got a little bit bigger, I explained to them, they were killed. Why they were killed? Because they were Jews, not because they were criminals or something. And that's why I was imprisoned. But you have related some of your personal details to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I've explained some of the personal, some of the background of the family as-- as time went on. And my son was receptive to it. My middle daughter was receptive to it to a degree. The youngest showed a little bit of rebellion. She did not want to accept it, officially. But yet, in secret, she would read up on it and look up. And we've caught her, many times, reading a book on the Holocaust or watching a  movie, which she should switch to the television not to try to show us.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Mr. Ziegler, we're going to give you a chance now to show  us some pictures and to talk about the  pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. OK. This is a picture of my parents. That picture is blown-up of a smaller one, which survived in the United States. The picture was sent in 1939 to my uncle, my mother's brother, in Baltimore, Maryland, which I retrieved after the war when I came to Canada. This is a picture of my oldest brother, Moishe Nukhem, which was taken in 1938 while serving with the Polish Army. This picture also survived with my uncle in the United States. This is a picture of myself, which was taken in the summer of 1939, as a schoolboy. I wasn't quite 12 yet. It also survived in the United States, with my uncle. This picture is of my brother Jacob and I. That was taken in the summer of 1947, almost a year before he died. There is a picture of myself, which was taken after the release from a Polish prison, in March of 1946. I am on the extreme right, sitting. This picture was taken in Welland in 1954. I have been already a Canadian citizen at the time. This is a picture of a monument, which is on the grave site of my sister who passed away a month after liberation in June, 1945. This is a picture taken in 1981 at the Yad Vashem, with the director of the Yad Vashem, Dr. Krakowski. This picture was also taken in 1981, while I was donating my number to the Yad Vashem. I was handing my number over to Gideon Hausner, the former prosecutor of Eichmann.
